publicationStmt.p: Transcribed from Bodleian Library, Lovelace Byron collection, Box 166, folios 119-121. Transcription by Lisa Gold (2022). TEI conversion for Sextant pipeline, 2026.

div.p: Thanks, my Dear, for your kind few lines of last evening. I am anxious to hear how your visits to Lord John & the Wilders went off tho'.

How kind is the enclosed from Henry Currie! I have answered it, expressing of course my sense of this. I have said too that I really would not scruple (as he lives so near) asking for the mare if at any time I do feel she would be a comfort to me for a short while. I have proposed that during the week after my return from hence, he & I should take a ride together, & that I might try the mare on that occasion. I should
just like to know what she is.--
I feel so very glad, my Dear, that you go visiting about, & being an affable social Crow. No crow ever was solitary!--
I shall like to pay some riding visits with you when I return, if I am as well as I expect.

You are a kind mate to feel so for me. I have indeed been in a most sad state. I think my organization is one of excessive acuteness in every item; & that sensations (when I have them at all) are of an extreme kind, either for pain or pleasure. This fact would account for my youthful tendency to over-eagerness as to even the most laudable objects.

The sensations over-ruled everything, until judgment experience & knowledge had taught me what the sensations were worth. At 30 one may have a considerable amount of knowledge experience and judgment, but at 20 one could have but little.

I am exceedingly struck with Miss Cosway. She has a defect in the palate, which mars her speech & hearing sadly; a modified degree of your Aunt Cath's. She is thus shut out from many outward enjoyments & advantages, & has become a very contemplative & thinking character. She is a little of a mathematician. Mrs King (who is vastly fastidious about young ladies, I don't mean religiously so however), says
she has scarcely ever met with so superior a young person. She has lived much with the Kings.--
Lady Caroline Sanford is here I belive, & I think I should call. I rather think that Lady Acland too is here. Mrs. Lamb had left before I arrived.

Thursdy Morning. A fine day, tho' windy. I am going out for the day, to make up for lost time in that line. I shall walk over to Shoreham Harbour, then row up the river there, & get two swims probably before evening. All this I manage quite quietly & incog.--
I think you will well like my swimming dress. The black colour, (combined with the slimness & boyish-ness of my
straight figure), make it really a graceful feminine-looking costume, & not the least startling.--
I have learned many things here as to conveniences for dress, for wet & windy weather, or climbing about &c.
I forgot to write you my applause of your reprobation of Miss Cooper's most sad dawdling. Pray brisken them up all you can, & encourage Dr C-- to do so too.
